content:
Most AI writing tools make impact stories sound like a press release. One didn’t.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Shubham Dhage on Unsplash
A friend who runs a small NGO sent me three versions of the same story last week.
Same beneficiary. Same outcome. Same facts.
One sounded like a corporate quarterly update. One sounded like a generic motivational quote. The third actually made me feel something.
All three were written by AI. Only one understood what nonprofit storytelling is actually supposed to do.

Why tone matters more than people think here
Nonprofit content isn’t like regular marketing copy. The whole point isn’t to sound impressive, it’s to make someone feel connected to a person they’ve never met.
The research backs this up clearly. Stories are remembered roughly 22 times more easily than facts and statistics alone, and according to data from DonorPerfect, nonprofits that use effective storytelling see 67% higher donor retention rates, and those that tell engaging stories raise twice as much as those that don’t.
So when an NGO uses a generic AI writing tool and gets back something that reads like a press release, it’s not just a stylistic miss. It’s actively working against the thing storytelling is supposed to achieve.

Tool 1: ChatGPT — flexible, but it needs a lot of steering
ChatGPT was the most adaptable of the three, but only once my friend learned how to prompt it properly.
Left with a vague instruction like “write this as an emotional story,” it produced something that felt close to AI-generated motivational poster text. Once she got specific — “write this like a field officer describing what she personally witnessed, not like a brochure” — the output improved dramatically.
The lesson here wasn’t about the tool’s limits. It was about how much prompting skill the result actually depends on, which isn’t reasonable to expect from someone whose actual job is fieldwork, not content writing.

Tool 2: Jasper — built for brand tone, not really for human tone
Jasper’s main selling point is its brand voice training, and on G2 it holds a strong reputation for this. One reviewer specifically praised how the tone and style customization options ensure content matches brand voice seamlessly.
But “brand voice” and “human, emotional voice” aren’t quite the same thing. Jasper is genuinely built for marketing consistency at scale, and reviewers note it can lean toward safe, predictable phrasing that reads like a competent first draft but lacks a point of view without heavy prompting. For a product description, that’s fine. For a story about a child who finally has access to clean water, “safe and predictable” is exactly the wrong register.

Tool 3: iMRIVE’s built-in AI Writing Assistant — the one that got it right
The version that actually moved me came from iMRIVE, a publishing platform built specifically for NGOs and educational institutions, with an AI Writing Assistant baked directly into the story editor.
The difference wasn’t magic. It was context. Because the tool is built only for impact storytelling, the tone options it offers — professional, storytelling, emotional, simple — are tuned for exactly this use case, instead of being a general-purpose writing assistant being awkwardly repurposed for it.
I want to be transparent here: iMRIVE doesn’t yet have a public review footprint on platforms like G2 or Capterra the way Jasper does, since it’s a newer, more focused platform. So I can’t point to independent verified reviews for it the way I did above. What I can say honestly is that, in this side-by-side test, the output needed the least editing to actually sound human — likely because the tool was never trying to be everything for everyone in the first place.

What I’d actually tell an NGO trying this themselves
A few honest takeaways from watching all three side by side:
General AI tools work, but only with very specific prompting. Don’t just say “make it emotional.” Describe the exact perspective you want — whose eyes is this story being told through.
Purpose-built tools need less correction, by design. A tool trained on one use case will usually outperform a general tool on that exact use case, even if the general tool is more powerful overall.
Always keep a human edit pass, no matter which tool you use. AI can structure a story well. It can’t know which detail about a real person actually mattered most. That part still needs you.
Lead with one person, not a statistic. Every version that worked, regardless of tool, started with a specific individual rather than an aggregate number.
Don’t skip the resolution. Nonprofit storytelling guides consistently flag that an unresolved or vague ending is one of the most common mistakes, and it’s the easiest thing to fix in an edit pass.

The takeaway
None of these tools are bad. They’re just built for different jobs, and tone is where that difference shows up the fastest.
If your content is meant to sell a product, a general AI writing tool with strong brand voice training will probably serve you well. If your content is meant to make someone feel a stranger’s story enough to open their wallet, a tool built specifically for that emotional register has a real structural advantage, even before you start editing.
For NGOs and small institutions specifically, that’s the gap worth knowing about before you pick a tool and assume the output will work itself out in editing.

I’m a student who got curious about this after watching a friend struggle to get any AI tool to sound right for her NGO’s stories. I want to be upfront that iMRIVE doesn’t have independent reviews to link to yet, so my take on it reflects direct testing, not a verified comparison. I’ve written before about a few India-built AI tools solving niche problems like this one, if this kind of comparison interests you.
More from this series on my Medium profile.
